questions.text: A market that is held on a specific day of the week is called a:
questions.explanation: Weekly markets do not have permanent shops; traders set up stalls for the day and close in the evening.

questions.text: Why are things often cheaper in weekly markets?
options: Traders don't have to pay permanent shop expenses like rent and electricity
questions.explanation: Shop owners in permanent buildings have many expenses that weekly market traders avoid.

questions.text: In weekly markets, most members of the ________ help the traders.
questions.explanation: Weekly market traders usually don't hire workers, saving on labour costs.

questions.text: A major advantage of a weekly market is that ________.
options: Most things you need are available in one place
It is open at night only
questions.explanation: Whether it's vegetables, groceries, or utensils, you can find them all together.

questions.text: Shops in our neighbourhood are useful because ________.
options: They are open on all days and close to our homes
questions.explanation: We can go there at any time during the week and they are usually reachable on foot.

questions.text: In neighbourhood shops, the buyer and seller usually ________ each other.
questions.explanation: This familiarity often allows buyers to get goods on credit.

questions.text: What does 'buying on credit' mean?
options: Paying with a credit card only
Paying for the goods at a later date
questions.explanation: Local shopkeepers often allow regular customers to pay after some time (e.g., at the end of the month).

questions.text: Large multi-storeyed air-conditioned buildings with shops on different floors are called:
questions.explanation: Shopping malls usually sell both branded and non-branded goods and are common in urban areas.

questions.text: Branded goods are often ________ compared to non-branded ones.
questions.explanation: Advertising and better packaging make branded goods more costly.

questions.text: The people who stand between the producer and the final consumer are the:
questions.explanation: Traders buy goods from producers and sell them to consumers.

questions.text: A ________ trader buys goods in large quantities.
questions.explanation: Wholesale traders buy directly from the producer or big factories and sell to other traders.

questions.text: The trader who finally sells the goods to the consumer is the:
questions.explanation: Retailers can be found in weekly markets, neighbourhood shops, or shopping malls.

questions.text: A 'Chain of Markets' starts from the ________ and ends with the ________.
questions.explanation: Goods travel through many hands before reaching the person who actually uses them.

questions.text: In the modern world, we can buy goods without ________.
questions.explanation: Orders can be placed via phone or internet, and items are delivered to our homes.

questions.text: Medical representatives who visit doctors are also a form of ________.
questions.explanation: They are involved in the process of selling medicines, though they don't have a traditional 'shop'.

questions.text: Which of these is NOT usually seen in a market?
questions.explanation: While construction materials are bought in a market, the construction itself is a separate activity.

questions.text: There are markets for items like fertilizers and seeds that we ________.
options: See every day in the city
May not even be aware of
questions.explanation: Farmers use these items which are sold in specific wholesale markets that ordinary city dwellers rarely visit.

questions.text: The income of a weekly market trader is ________ compared to a shop owner in a mall.
questions.explanation: Small traders earn very little, while mall shop owners make huge profits.

questions.text: Markets also depend on the ________ level of the buyers.
questions.explanation: Whether we buy from a weekly market or a mall depends largely on how much money we have.

questions.text: A 'Complex' in a city usually refers to ________.
options: A place with many shops in the same area
questions.explanation: Urban areas often have these specialized shopping zones.

questions.text: The main role of the 'Wholesaler' is to ________.
options: Store goods and sell to other traders
questions.explanation: They act as a bulk storage and supply point in the chain of markets.

questions.text: Weekly markets allow people to ________ to get a lower price.
questions.explanation: Bargaining is common because many traders sell the same goods, creating competition.

questions.text: An example of goods sold in large quantities is ________.
questions.explanation: Wholesale traders deal in massive volumes (quintals/tons) that a single consumer wouldn't need.

questions.text: Every city has several ________ areas.
questions.explanation: These are areas where goods first arrive and are then distributed to other traders.

questions.text: Which of these provides 'convenience' to the consumer?
questions.explanation: Proximity to home and flexibility in timing and payment make them convenient.

questions.text: A 'Consumer' is a person who ________.
questions.explanation: The consumer is the final link in the economic chain.

questions.text: Why do mall shops have high prices?
options: High rent, air-conditioning, and branding costs
They don't want to sell anything
questions.explanation: The luxurious setting and high operational costs are passed on to the customers.

questions.text: A cloth merchant buys cloth from a ________.
questions.explanation: He is part of the chain that brings clothing from the weaver to the wearer.

questions.text: Internet shopping uses ________ to buy products.
questions.explanation: E-commerce has made markets accessible from anywhere.

questions.text: The weekly market trader's shop is ________.
questions.explanation: They set up in the morning and pack up in the evening.

questions.text: Neighbourhood shops can be ________.
options: Roadside stalls like a vegetable hawker
questions.explanation: They include everything from a dairy to a fruit juice cart nearby.

questions.text: Bargaining is more likely to happen in a ________.
questions.explanation: Fixed prices are common in malls, whereas weekly markets allow for negotiation.

questions.text: A 'Link' in the market chain refers to ________.
questions.explanation: Each trader is a link that passes the product closer to the consumer.

questions.text: A 'Retailer' can be ________.
questions.explanation: Any person who sells directly to the consumer is a retailer.

questions.text: Is there inequality in markets?
options: Yes, in terms of earnings and access
questions.explanation: Big traders earn much more than small ones, and some people cannot afford mall prices.

questions.text: Advertising aims to make branded goods look ________.
questions.explanation: This increases demand and justifies the higher price tag.

questions.text: Farmers often buy their supplies in ________ markets.
questions.explanation: These are specialized markets serving the agricultural sector.

questions.text: Weekly markets provide ________ for local producers to sell their goods.
questions.explanation: Small farmers and artisans use these markets to reach buyers directly.

questions.text: Which of these is a 'hidden' market activity?
options: A trader selling to a retailer
questions.explanation: Most buying and selling happens before a product ever reaches a retail shop shelf.

questions.text: Convenience, price, and choice are three factors why we ________.
questions.explanation: These factors determine where we decide to shop.
